coredata.dc:title: Facile and label-free fluorescence sensing of β-galactosidase activity by graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xiangjun
Lian, Yawen
Xiao, Lehui
Wei, Lin
coredata.dc:description: β-Galactosidase (β-Gal), as a glycoside hydrolase, is closely associated with cell senescence and primary ovarian cancer. However, there is still lack of facile and rapid sensing approach to monitor the β-Gal activity. In this work, a label-free and convenient sensing strategy to detect β-Gal activity has been proposed based on fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). In the presence of β-Gal, 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-galactopyranoside (NPGal) can be hydrolyzed into 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), which serves as a good quencher to quench the fluorescence of GQDs. The quenching mechanism is proven to be inner filter effect (IFE). Due to the specificity of the enzymatic reaction, this sensing method displays excellent selectivity and high sensitivity. A broad dynamic range from 20 to 200 U L−1 and a detection limit of 4.4 U L−1 for the β-Gal assay are achieved. Compared with the previously reported methods, this sensing strategy only needs one fluorescent nanomaterial without any modification and avoids time-consuming handling steps. Therefore, the sensing strategy based on fluorescent GQDs offers great potential for the recognition of disease-correlated enzyme activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-Galactosidase
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence sensing
Inner filter effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), (b), (c) and (d) are the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence, FT-IR and XPS spectra of GQDs. (e) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs with variations excitation wavelength. (f) TEM image of GQDs. scale bar is 20 nm.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP (green line) and excitation spectrum of GQDs (red line). (b) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs (blank line), GQDs and NPGal (blue line), GQDs and 4-NP (green line), GQDs, NPGal and β-Gal (red line).

(a) Fluorescence response of GQDs upon addition of various concentrations of 4-NP (from 0 to 90 μM) and (b) fitting curve between quenched luminescence and 4-NP concentrations in the range from 1.0 to 80.0 μM. (c) Fluorescence quenching vs incubation time at different β-Gal levels (100, 150, 260 U L−1) of the sensing system. (d) Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system including GQDs, NPGal and β-Gal at different β-Gal levels from 0 to 270 U L−1. (e) Fitting curve between quenched fluorescence and β-Gal activity in the range from 20 to 200 U L−1. (f) Fluorescence decay of GQDs (black line) and GQDs in the presence of 4-NP (red line).
Selectivity evaluation of the sensing assay toward β-Gal.
Schematic illustration of the sensing principle for β-Gal activity detection based on GQDs.

head.ce:title: Facile and label-free fluorescence sensing of β-galactosidase activity by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: β-Galactosidase (β-Gal), as a glycoside hydrolase, is closely associated with cell senescence and primary ovarian cancer. However, there is still lack of facile and rapid sensing approach to monitor the β-Gal activity. In this work, a label-free and convenient sensing strategy to detect β-Gal activity has been proposed based on fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). In the presence of β-Gal, 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-galactopyranoside (NPGal) can be hydrolyzed into 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), which serves as a good quencher to quench the fluorescence of GQDs. The quenching mechanism is proven to be inner filter effect (IFE). Due to the specificity of the enzymatic reaction, this sensing method displays excellent selectivity and high sensitivity. A broad dynamic range from 20 to 200 U L−1 and a detection limit of 4.4 U L−1 for the β-Gal assay are achieved. Compared with the previously reported methods, this sensing strategy only needs one fluorescent nanomaterial without any modification and avoids time-consuming handling steps. Therefore, the sensing strategy based on fluorescent GQDs offers great potential for the recognition of disease-correlated enzyme activity.

ce:section.ce:para: β-Galactosidase (β-Gal) is a glycoside hydrolase which can catalyze the hydrolysis of β-glycosides via cleaving the glycosidic bond [1,2]. Earlier studies have demonstrated that it could be adopted as a promising molecular marker for cell senescence and primary ovarian cancer [3–5]. It has been reported that β-Gal exhibits abnormal level in senescent cells. However, β-Gal deficiency would lead to β-galactosialidosis or Morquio B syndrome [6,7]. Thus, development of effective detection method with high sensitivity and selectivity to monitor β-Gal activity has aroused more and more interests. Recently, several intriguing approaches for β-Gal detection have been proposed including positron emission tomography (PET), colorimetric sensing, single photoemission computed tomography (SPECT), fluorescent analysis and so on [8–12]. Among these methods, fluorescent detection using fluorescent probes stands out because of its excellent sensitivity and selectivity, low cost, and simple handling step. Until now, only few fluorescent probes have been reported to detect β-Gal activity. For example, Kim and coworkers proposed a two-photon fluorescent probe by ratiometric analysis to monitor β-Gal activity in cellular senescence period [13]. Liu et al. constructed a fluorescent probe named SA-βGal with light-up response to β-Gal [14]. However, most fluorescent sensors contain fluorophore with unstable optical properties and also suffer from complex synthesis route. Thus, it is necessary and significant to exploit novel fluorescent probe for β-Gal activity detection.

In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have aroused great interests due to the unique and stable optical properties, facile preparation process, easy surface functionalization, favorable water dispersibility and biocompatibility [15–19]. Moreover, as a new form of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials [20–26], GQDs exhibit enormous superiorities in ion sensing, molecular recognition, and imaging analysis [27–29]. The dominant fluorescence of GQDs derives from their remarkable quantum confinement and edge effects. And most sensing applications of the GQDs are based on changes in their fluorescent signals [30–32]. For instance, Doong et al. synthesized high quantum yield GQDs and detected Fe3+ ions in aqueous media [33]. Liu group developed a kind of GQDs-based fluorescent probe for sensing ascorbic acid [28]. Nigam and coworkers chose GQDs coupled with functionalized human serum albumin nanoparticles for specific drug delivery and imaging of pancreatic cancer [34]. In terms of these sensing systems, the majority of works are based on fluorescence on/off switching from GQDs. The interactions between GQDs and analytes made the fluorescence of GQDs being quenched. Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of GQDs mainly include Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), photoinduced charge transfer (PCT) and inner filter effect (IFE) [35,36]. The FRET process involves in the relative orientation of the dipoles, overlap extent of the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum and the distance between them. Since the distance as one of the important influence factor in FRET process, surface-chemical modification of GQDs is usually necessary. The PET mechanism can be considered as an internal redox reaction process between the fluorophore and analyte. A new compound is usually formed and the excited electrons are returned to the ground state. Moreover, there is no photon emission in the whole process. The PCT refers to the electron transfer between donor and acceptor. The complexation of donor and acceptor would cause the alteration of electron energy level, and associate with the change of fluorescence signals. The IFE generally relates to the absorption spectrum of the analyte and the fluorescence spectra of the probe. When the absorption band of the analyte overlaps with the excitation or emission bands from the fluorescent probe, the IFE process would take place and the fluorescence of the probe is quenched. Different from FRET, IFE also happens even when the distance between the donor and the acceptor is more than 10 nm. Thus, this detection process does not require complicated functionalization process for the fluorescent probe. Besides, there is no new compound formed in IFE process and the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophores will not change. Therefore, the mechanism in many sensing systems based on unmodified carbon quantum dots is attributed to IFE [37,38].

In this work, fluorescent GQDs are used as a facile and effective probe for the sensitive and selective detection of β-Gal activity. In this design, β-Gal activates the hydrolysis of the 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-galactopyranoside (NPGal), which is considered as the common substrate of β-Gal. The products of hydrolysis contain galactose and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). The latter could quench the fluorescence of GQDs, and the quenching mechanism is ascribed to the IFE process. The absorption spectrum of 4-NP can well overlap with the excitation spectrum of GQDs. In the presence of β-Gal, the excitation of GQDs is significantly weakened by the competitive absorption reaction, leading to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs. The limit of detection (LOD) of this assay is 4.4 U L−1. Compared with other detection methods, this sensing system does not need expensive instruments and complicated modification procedures. Therefore, this detection strategy provided new insights on the application of GQDs to monitor enzyme activity.

β-Galactosidase (β-Gal, EC 3.2.1.23, 25 kU), and 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-galactopyranoside (NPGal) were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science &Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), lysine, MgCl2, AlCl3, NaCl, KCl and CaCl2, glutathione (GSH), glucose oxidase (GOx), sodium hydroxide, citric acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and other reagents were acquired from Sinopharm Group Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the reagents in the whole experiment were analytical grade and used directly without further purification.

The fluorescent excitation and emission spectra were performed on F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis spectra were recorded with a spectrometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEM-2100 apparatus with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs was measured on Thermo Nicolet 6700 Fouier-transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained by K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes of the GQDs were measured on fluorescence time-correlated single photon counting spectrophotometer (FluoroCube-3000U, Horiba Scientific, France).

The GQDs were synthesized on the basis of previous work [39]. In brief, 0.2 g solid-state citric acid was added into a stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C in an oven. During the process, the citric acid was melted gradually. The color of the solution underwent from colorless to yellowish and finally orange, indicating the formation of GQDs. After heating about 20 min, NaOH solution (10 mg mL−1) was introduced into the mixture and a yellowish solution acquired. At last, pH of the prepared GQDs was adjusted to neutral by acid solution and stored in 4 °C before use.

In order to evaluate the fluorescence quenching result of GQDs by 4-NP, herein, 100 μL of pure 4-NP solution was added into the prepared GQDs solution (1 mL) at room temperature. The optimal quenching reaction time was measured from 1 to 8 min. Moreover, the quenching efficiency of the 4-NP was also assessed. Under the optimal reaction time, the influences on fluorescence intensity of GQDs at different 4-NP concentrations (0.0–90.0 μM) were measured.

The β-Gal activity was assessed by increasing reaction time at a certain enzyme concentration. Firstly, 100 μL NPGal (100 μM) in PBS buffer was preheated at 37 °C. Then, three different concentrations of β-Gal (100, 150, 260 U L−1) were introduced to the solution and incubated at 37 °C, respectively. After that, 1 mL GQDs was added to this solution. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the sensing system were recorded every minute for 10 min.

The optical properties of as-prepared GQDs were characterized by UV–vis, FL and FT-IR spectra respectively as depicted in Fig. 1 . The GQDs exhibit a strong optical absorption at about 230 nm, which is ascribed to π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain of CC bonds. Moreover, there is a shoulder peak in the range of 330–370 nm owing to n-π* transition of CO bonds (Fig. 1a) [40]. The fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of the GQDs locate at about 370 and 475 nm (Fig. 1b). Based on the excellent dispersity in aqueous solution, we speculated that the GQDs are covered by hydrophilic groups, including carbonyl, hydroxyl and amine group. The FT-IR spectrum also confirms this argument. As displayed in Fig. 1c, the peaks at 3440 and 1637 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of -OH and CO bond, respectively. The absorption bands appeared at 1381 and 1084 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CH and CC bonds. These absorption peaks in FT-IR spectrum certify the functional groups on the surface of GQDs and also agree with their good solubility. In addition, XPS analysis indicated that the GQDs are composed of C, O and Na elements (Fig. 1d). The Na1s peak can be considered from NaOH. It can be seen from Fig. 1e that the GQDs show excitation-dependent property. TEM measurement clearly manifests the narrow diameter distribution of GQDs (3.1 ± 0.9 nm) as shown in Fig. 1f.

The detection process of β-Gal activity based on GQDs is shown in Scheme 1 . Herein, GQDs without any modification were directly used. The detection mechanism is that the fluorescence of GQDs could be quenched by 4-NP, which is derived from the hydrolysis product of β-glycosides by β-Gal. According to the quenching mechanism mentioned above, we speculated that the fluorescence of GQDs might be quenched due to the IFE. In a general way, IFE happens when the absorption band of the quencher overlapped with the fluorescence spectra of the probe. In the β-Gal activity detection system, the absorption spectrum of 4-NP partially overlaps with the excitation spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2a). When 4-NP was introduced into the system, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched effectually. The measurement of fluorescence emission spectra further revealed these results. As shown in Fig. 2b, there is only slight effect on the fluorescence of GQDs after the addition of NPGal. But the fluorescence intensity of the solution decreased rapidly after 4-NP was added. The obvious difference from the quenching results offers the possibility to monitor β-Gal activity. As expected, when β-Gal and NPGal were added, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched effectively. Therefore, it is practicable to monitor β-Gal activity by GQDs.

Recently, 4-NP has been employed as an efficient quencher to detect metal ions and small molecules based on carbon dots [38], metal quantum dots [41] and aggregation-induced emission nanoparticles [11]. Herein, the quenching efficiency of 4-NP on the fluorescence of GQDs was also explored. According to Fig. 3a, gradual fluorescence quenching tendency of GQDs at different concentrations of 4-NP was examined. This observation implied that 4-NP could quench the fluorescence of GQDs efficiently. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching exhibited good linear relationship and its correlation coefficient reached 0.995 (Fig. 3b).

After proving the effective quenching capability of 4-NP to the fluorescence of GQDs, the performance of β-Gal activity detection was also evaluated. In this work, NPGal was used as the substrate for β-Gal. Under the catalysis of β-Gal, NPGal can be readily hydrolyzed to 4-NP. In addition, the reaction time is a critical parameter for enzyme reaction. Thus, optimization of the reaction time for the fluorescence quenching of GQDs in the existence of three different concentrations of β-Gal was performed and shown in Fig. 3c. It was found that the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly and a plateau was achieved after 6 min, suggesting the completion of the catalysis reaction based on β-Gal. Then we figured out the influence of different concentrations of β-Gal on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. The fluorescence intensity change also indicates a good linear relationship (Fig. 3d and e). This result clearly reveals the correlation between the fluorescence of GQDs and β-Gal activity in the range from 20 to 200 U L−1 and the limit of detection is confirmed to be 4.4 U L−1. These results also prove the sensing strategy is convenient and reliable. Fig. 3f displays the unchanged fluorescence lifetime of GQDs even in the presence of 4-NP, which further proves the IFE mechanism for β-Gal detection.

To evaluate the specificity of the sensing assay, various species were chosen to verify the selectivity and specificity. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence quenching efficiency of β-Gal is excellent, while there is no obvious fluorescence change after the addition of other ions or molecules even when their concentrations reached 0.1 mM or 200 U L−1. Because of the specificity of enzymatic reaction, the assay based on β-Gal-catalyzed reaction displayed excellent selectivity and specificity.

As one of cancer biomarkers, β-Gal is very important to human body doubtlessly. Thus, development of fast and simple method to monitor the β-Gal activity is significant. In this work, we demonstrate a facile and label-free fluorescence sensing approach for β-Gal activity detection by GQDs. In this system, GQDs played as a fluorophore and the hydrolysate of β-Gal named 4-NP as an absorber. The fluorescence of GQDs can be effectively quenched by 4-NP base on the IFE mechanism. This sensing system proves that GQDs can be used as efficient probe for β-Gal detection. Compared with other methods, this design displays favorable performances but avoids complicated operations, which can be further extended to other biomolecule detection in the future.
Xiangjun Xie: Investigation. Yawen Lian: Investigation. Lehui Xiao: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Lin Wei: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Excellent Youth Scholars of Hunan Province Education Department (17B155).
